coredata.dc:title: Hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites in novel drug delivery platforms: Recent trends and developments
coredata.prism:publicationName: Advances in Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Amiri, Mahnaz
Khazaeli, Payam
Salehabadi, Ali
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
coredata.dc:description:
The present article evaluates the composition and synthesis of hydrogel beads. Hydrogels, owing to their known biocompatibility, are widely used in drug delivery as a host (or drug carrier). Hydrogels, owing to their physical, chemical and biological properties, are popular in many aspects. Hydrogels are crosslinked-hydrophilic polymers and commercialized/synthesized in both natural and synthetic forms. These polymers are compatible with human tissues, therefore can be potentially used for biomedical treatments. Hydrogels in drug delivery offer several points of interest such as sustainability, and sensitivity without any side-effects as compared to traditional methods in this field. Drugs can encapsulate and release continuously into the targets when hydrogels are activated/modified magnetically or by fluorescent materials. It is crucial to develop new crosslinked polymers in terms of “biocompatibility” and “biodegradability” for novel drug delivery platforms. In the event that the accomplishments of the past can be used into the longer terms, it is exceedingly likely that hydrogels with a wide cluster of alluring properties can be synthesized. The current review, offers an updated summary of latest developments in the nanomedicines field as well as nanobased drug delivery systems over broad study of the discovery/ application of nanomaterials in improving both the efficacy of drugs and targeted delivery of them. The challenges/opportunities of nanomedicine in drug delivery also discussed.
Scope of the research Although several reviews have been published in the field of hydrogels, however many of them have just centralized on the general overviews in terms of “synthesis” and “properties”. The utilization of hydrogels and hydrogel-based composites in vital applications have been achieved a great interest. In this review, our aim is to recap of the key points in the field of hydrogels such as; a) hydrogel nanocomposites, b) magnetic beads, c) biomedical applications, and d) drug delivery. In the same vein, these outlines will be expanded with emphasizing on the boon of magnetic beads and recent developments in this area.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present article evaluates the composition and synthesis of hydrogel beads. Hydrogels, owing to their known biocompatibility, are widely used in drug delivery as a host (or drug carrier). Hydrogels, owing to their physical, chemical and biological properties, are popular in many aspects. Hydrogels are crosslinked-hydrophilic polymers and commercialized/synthesized in both natural and synthetic forms. These polymers are compatible with human tissues, therefore can be potentially used for biomedical treatments. Hydrogels in drug delivery offer several points of interest such as sustainability, and sensitivity without any side-effects as compared to traditional methods in this field. Drugs can encapsulate and release continuously into the targets when hydrogels are activated/modified magnetically or by fluorescent materials. It is crucial to develop new crosslinked polymers in terms of “biocompatibility” and “biodegradability” for novel drug delivery platforms. In the event that the accomplishments of the past can be used into the longer terms, it is exceedingly likely that hydrogels with a wide cluster of alluring properties can be synthesized. The current review, offers an updated summary of latest developments in the nanomedicines field as well as nanobased drug delivery systems over broad study of the discovery/ application of nanomaterials in improving both the efficacy of drugs and targeted delivery of them. The challenges/opportunities of nanomedicine in drug delivery also discussed.

Although several reviews have been published in the field of hydrogels, however many of them have just centralized on the general overviews in terms of “synthesis” and “properties”. The utilization of hydrogels and hydrogel-based composites in vital applications have been achieved a great interest. In this review, our aim is to recap of the key points in the field of hydrogels such as; a) hydrogel nanocomposites, b) magnetic beads, c) biomedical applications, and d) drug delivery. In the same vein, these outlines will be expanded with emphasizing on the boon of magnetic beads and recent developments in this area.

ce:section.ce:para: Spinel ferrite (SF) - a complex metal oxide and magnetic nanocarriers – and hydrogels – crosslinked hydrophilic polymers - are two crucial materials in (bio)medical applications [1]. Hydrogel compounds are porous, safe, hydrophile, environmentally sensitive, and biocompatible in many academic and industrial applications [2–4]. Hydrogels are compatible with vital tissues owing to their hydrophilicity nature and water content. This aptitude is due to the existence of the hydrophile functional groups in the polymeric backbone. The barrier performance of the hydrogels against dissolution arises from the networks of the continues chains. Hydrogels can be obtained either naturally or synthetically, however, over the past few decades, synthetic hydrogels have been successfully stablished owing to their superior performances in terms of “water uptake”, “service lives”, and “gel strength”. In addition, synthetic hydrogels are stable against fluctuation of temperature [5].

Hydrogels are known as multi-component systems where composed in 3D polymeric networks. In this network, there is two major compartments; polymer chains and water, where water molecules are in the spaces between polymer chains [6]. Wichterle et al. in 1954 [7] reported the first founding in production of synthetic hydrogels. Nowadays, synthetic hydrogels are widely used in hygienic products [8], agriculture [9], drug delivery systems [10], coal dewatering [11], food additives [12], pharmaceuticals [13], biomedical applications [14,15] tissue engineering and regenerative medicines [16,17], diagnostics [18], wound dressing [19], separation of biomolecules or cells [20], barrier materials to regulate biological adhesions [21], and Biosensor [22].

Alginate is natural-based polysaccharide with linear backbone consisting of 1,4-linked β-D-mannuronic acid (M-block) and α-L-glucuronic acid (G-block) [23–24]. It can form chelate in the presence of A2+ (divalent ions) in order to create hydrogel beads in three types, based on the junctions of G- and M-blocks, including GG/GG, MG/MG, GG/MG junctions [25]. Formation of strong junctions between G-block and divalent cations can lead creation of hydrogel beads [26]. The faster crosslinking rate of Fe3+ ions with hydrogel beads could be a significant benefit for higher drug loading in the hydrogel beads [27]. The hydrogel beads crosslinked with Fe3+ display sustained drug release in simulated intestinal fluid compared with those crosslinked with divalent metal ions because of three-dimensional network structure formation. The interaction between alginate and multivalent cations lead formation of firm hydrogels. Fig. 1 indicates the SEM photographs of metformin loaded hydrogel beads before and after drug release [28]. When hydrogel beads are exposed to multivalent cations, many side interactions form new crosslinked materials. This characteristic crosslinking behavior with multivalent cations is key to the formation of alginate hydrogels. Crosslinking between alginate and many other active polymers are reported for fabrication of novel active hydrogels [29–31]. Alginate polymer has been widely used in biomedical applications e.g., wound dressing [32], scaffolds [33], and dental or surgical impression materials [34] as well as drug delivery systems [35].

Chitosan is biopolymer which is categorized under N-Acetylglucosaminea. It is structured in a cationic platform consisted of amine (−NH2) and hydroxyl (-OH) groups. In the structure of chitosan, in addition to N-acetyl-D-glucosamine (C8H15NO6), β1-D-glucosamine and β4-D-glucosamine are randomly distributed [36]. Chitosan is a bio-sustainable polymer in terms compatibility and degradability, nontoxic, high gel-contents, and pH-sensitive appropriates for modern drug delivery technologies. Though many biopolymers and their respective derivatives are utilized in drug-controlled release [37–40], however cationic polymers like chitosan, owing to their ability to form gel beads with spherical morphology, have been achieved more interests. The electrostatic interactions between polycationic and polyanionic compounds govern their wide range applications in this field. Gel-beads chitosan is recently used as a smart drug carrier due to its easy processability, uniform shape and small size [41,42]. However, the gel-beads can degrade immediately in the gastrointestinal tract, which can further culminate in initiation of high content drug release [43]. In this criterion, and due to the urgent biomedicine needs, several new materials-based hydrogels have been developed with improved functionalities and physicochemical properties. The current improvements are induced by modification of hydrogels in terms of “nanocomposite formation” and “core-shell” technologies, for example the formation based mineral-NPs/hydrogel nanocomposites [44]. Clay, graphene oxide (GO), metal oxide nanoparticles (MONPs), and metal nanoparticles (MNPs) are some examples of mineral-NPs in hydrogels [45–51]. Digital captures of pristine hydrogel beads-based chitosan, and CH/CuO/hydrogel nanocomposites are presented in Fig. 2 . The presence of mineral-NPs can also prevent the uncontrolled initiation of drug release. In the literatures, and in many aspects, the exact profiles of drug release are investigated including hydrogel containing clay, hydroxyapatite, CuO, MgO, etc. [52–56]. In the same vein, crosslinked-hydrogel based nanocomposites plays important roles in the interaction profiles (Fig. 3 ) [57–60]. The interactions between nanoclay (NC) and hydrogel are widely investigated, either theoretically or experimentally. Fig. 4 shows a proposed model of hydrogel/NC nanocomposites in the vein of sustainable conformation/formation.

Heteropolymers can be classified based on the polymeric compositions. These classifications are listed below:
a)
Homopolymer – is a basic structure of any polymer, including a network of single monomer [61]. It can crosslink in order to form polymer backbone. In backbone formation, the nature of the monomer, and selected polymerization method play crucial roles.
b)
Copolymer – consists of at least two different monomers and one hydrophilic compartment. In this polymer, the polymeric skeleton can be arranged either randomly, or in block. In addition, it can be configured alternatively along the polymer chain [62].
c)
Interpenetrating polymeric network (IPN) – embraces of two crosslinked polymers (natural, synthetic). There are two classes of IPN; normal IPN (two crosslinked polymers), and semi-IPN (one crosslinked and one non-crosslinked polymer) [63,64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polymeric beads: preparation strategies and methods
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned before, hydrogels are fabricated from both naturally occurring and synthetic materials in a 3D network of polymer chains with high water/fluid absorption capacity [65]. The hydrophile functional groups like hydroxyl (-OH), primary and secondary amide (-CO-NH-), and sulfonyl hydroxide (-SO2OH) can impart more hydrophilicity in the hydrogels, while physical crosslinking affects physical integrity. The physical crosslinking occurs via; a) hydrophobic-, hydrogen-, ionic-, physical domain- interactions [65], while chemical crosslinking governs by covalent bonding in the presence of crosslinking agent, for example ethylene glycol and divinylbenzene [66]. The physical crosslinking plays a crucial role in hydrogel network formation. This is known as reversible (physical) gels, since a secondary force or molecular entanglements are effective. Physical gels can be dissolved in its surrounding environment. The extent of dissolution can be altered by variation of pH and/or temperature. In both dry and wet media, the chemical gels can be polymerized by covalent bonding of macromolecules with crosslinked polymers [67]. There are two classes of chemical gels; charged or non-charged. This depends on the nature of structural functional groups. In charged hydrogels, variation of pH can affect the swelling, which can further undergo shape-changed in electrical fields [68]. Chemical hydrogels are frequently fabricated via two methods; 3D polymerization and direct crosslinking. In 3D polymerization, there are two major compartments; a hydrophile monomer, and a crosslinking agent, while in direct method, there are hydrophile polymers. Generally, the 3D polymerization suffers from intact monomers after polymerization, therefore, time by time purifications are required, in order to remove the toxic monomers from the bulk. In Table 1 , the major physical and chemical crosslinked hydrogels are presented by emphasizing the types of polymers, method of preparations, and loaded drugs [69–74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biomedical applications of nanocomposite based polymeric beads

ce:section.ce:para: Lately, antibacterial hydrogels have been received attentions in recent years, owing to their enhanced biomedical potential. In antibacterial hydrogels, inorganic/organic (hydrogels) nanocomposites are more favorable in terms of “processing” and “bacterial inactivation”. The existence of antibacterial agents in the hydrogels inhibits microbial growth. The antimicrobial hydrogels can be rapidly functionalized in the presence of bioactive and inorganic materials, therefore suited for smart biomedical applications [75]. Chitosan itself does not show any antibacterial action at pH = 7.0; to convey this trait, externally incorporation of antibacterial agents is suggested [76]. The incorporation of nanoscales metal (such as AgNPs, AuNPs) and metal oxides (like ZnO) in chitosan has been widely investigated in terms of their “antibacterial activities” [77]. In the same vein, low-cost copper oxide nanoparticles (CuONPs) are successfully examined as an effective antibacterial material in hydrogels, which are sustainable, safe, stable (either physically or chemically), and reactive against polymers. In chitosan/CuO nanocomposites, the CuONPs can be attached bacteria, and damaged the cell-wall. This can be finally caused cell-death [78]. Fig. 5 represents how chitosan interacts with copper and CuONPs [44]. In this regard, in our laboratory, we successfully examined the anti-tumor properties of a mixed metal oxide based CuCo2O4/CuO [79].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanocomposites based hydrogels for drug delivery
ce:section.ce:para: Multidisciplinary nanoscales materials including layered materials [80,81], nanotubes [82], metal oxides [83] and, complex organic-inorganic materials [84] are investigated/reported in fabrication of novel nanocomposite-based hydrogel beads. As mentioned before, hydrogels are potential for drug delivery owing to their unique physicochemical properties [85–87]. In the same vein, a schematic representation of the fabrication pathways of hydrogels/Fe2O3 nanocomposites is presented in Fig. 6 , where the overall processes are carried out at the interface of the water droplet, in n-hexane, and in the presence of sodium alginate (gelation agent) [83]. The level of permeability of hydrogels governs by the extent of crosslinking and hydrophilicity. The surface and bulk porosities play additional roles in loading/releasing of drugs. Fig. 7 shows the drug release mechanism of CMC/ZnO bionanocomposites [43]. The drug can be stacked into hydrogels, where it can discharge continuously via swelling, and diffusion. The diffusion frameworks can be followed by store or lattice gadgets. Both allow discharging over hydrogel pores. A store (reservoir) conveyance framework contains a core (drug) and a shell (hydrogel), available in various forms such as chunks, capsules, etc. In the assumed frameworks, the central concentrations of drug are higher, in order to discharge constantly [88]. Primarily, the drugs are dissolved/dispersed in the matrix in 3D structural nature of the hydrogel, and released through the pores. The initial release rate depends on the square root of time, which is known as Case II transport (i.e. time-independent kinetics of release). Case II transport is anomalous transport i.e. swelling-controlled release + diffusion [89]. The current slope between the scattered medicate within the hydrogel and the encompassing environment allows the dissemination of the stacked dynamic fixing, from the concentration through the hydrogel [90]. The molar flux (J) of the drug (Eq.1) is directly related to the concentration gradient (Δc); J = −D × Δc (1); where D is diffusion coefficient (cm2/s). Time and the kinetic of release can be measured via Eq.2; dc = dt = −Δ × J = Δ × (D × Δc) (2); which corresponds to the “drug transport” of the hydrogel, while the border is a static [91]. The hydrogels can be shaped depending on the route of drug delivery. These interesting applications of hydrogels can be used to carry drugs that can help decrease the signs of many pathological situations. For example, the admixture of PVA and PVP are used for atopic dermatitis treatment by Nhoet al. [92], where primarily dissolved in herbal extract producing gel. Finally, the physical gel is preserved/sterilized by radiations for initiation of chemical crosslinking. It is crucial to synchronize the drug delivery and the physiological status of the patient, in response to environmental changes. The control release of drugs ate important particularly when drugs show some side effects. Swelling properties of the hydrogels can be altered with respect to their thermal, structural, and mechanical properties, when react with internal/external motives [93]. Bae et al. [94] recommended a delivery device for releasing drug from hydrogels, based on physical and chemical stimulations. The hydrogel in this field should be pH and temperature sensitive such as crosslinked homo−/co-polymers. The proposed structure reported by Bae and his coworkers are composed of a porous gel limited in a walled structure penetrable to the loaded drug. Consequently, a self-drug delivery occurs whenever it is needed.

The term “smart” is recently used for hydrogels, particularly with sensitivity against environment. As mentioned before (Section 3.1), various physical and chemical motivations are used to modify the selectivity and sensitivity of the selected hydrogel. Smart hydrogels are widely investigated in artificial tissues engineering, bioseperation, cells and enzymatic reactions. The most commonly used hydrogels are probably temperature-sensitive hydrogels as a class of environmentally sensitive polymer systems in drug delivery research, but nowadays the ability of magneto-sensitive hydrogels have increased their use.

As expressed over, hydrogels are known as 3D polymer, with hydrophilic nature, which are competent of continuous water absorption ↔ desorption, which can hold a huge volume of fluid within the swollen state. This class of polymers can be arranged with controllable reactions as to shrivel or grow with adjustments in outside natural conditions. Hydrogels may perform sensational volume move in reaction to differences of physical and chemical boosts, where the physical jolts incorporate temperature, electric or attractive field, light, weight, and sound, whereas the chemical boosts incorporate pH, dissolvable composition, ionic quality, and atomic species. The degree of swelling or de-swelling in response to the varieties in the outside environment of the hydrogel can be so exceptional that the marvel is alluded to as volume collapse or stage move [95]. Magnetic hydrogels have been achieved great interest in recent years, by demonstrating “sensitivity” to an external magnetic field (EMF) as stimuli. This is a characteristic of magnetic hydrogels intransitive for drug delivery and biomedical applications [96]. The advent of nanotechnology in fabrication of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) has changed the technological overview of the hydrogels in terms of “magnetic beads” [97]. MNPs display magnetization, which is remarked as “suitable” for various applications, particularly in the presence of external magnetic fields. Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) are inorganic and zero-dimensional materials with a metal-based configuration. These NPs have found increasing importance as they can be easily manipulated using an alternating magnetic field (AMF) and, subsequently, be employed in several applications. Nanometersized MNPs have revealed intrinsic and unique properties such as high saturation magnetization (Ms), less toxicity and biocompatibility. Accordingly, MNPs have been applied in various fields such as industrial, environmental, analytical, and biomedical applications [98–100]. In particular, MNPs have gained attention for biomedical applications because of their easy controllability, biological compatibility, physicochemical properties, and superior magnetic properties. Biomedical applications depend on the MNPs properties which can be affected by synthesis methods, particle size distribution, interaction between particles, as well as particle size and shape of NPs. Therefore, size and shape represent the key features governing the physical stability of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs). As demonstrated in various papers, size can strongly influence the magnetic property and subsequent response to magnetic fields. For example, when the size shrinks, the saturation magnetization of iron oxide particles declines, while the particles can exhibit supermagnetism, which confers greater capabilities of magnetization compared to the abilities of paramagnetic materials. Therefore, a suitable synthesis method should be selected to attain a specific performance (based on certain biomedical applications).In this regard, the last ten years have witnessed the development of a wide diversity of nanofabrication techniques fulfilling high expectations surrounding nanotechnology and nanofabrication. Numerous methods have been used for the synthesis of magnetic nanoparticles. These include: Mechanical milling, Co-precipitation, Nanoreactors/microemulsion techniques, Sonochemical processing, Sol-gel methods, Flow injection, Electrochemical production, Supercritical fluid techniques, Thermal decomposition, Hydrothermal routes, Microwave techniques, Spray pyrolysis, Laser pyrolysis, Flame spray pyrolysis, Gas phase synthesis, Arc discharge, Oxidation, Microbial methods [101–103]. MNPs such as magnetite (Fe3O4) are recently promoted in various vital applications including biosensing devices, targeting technologies, etc. magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [104–107]. MNPs can incorporate into alginate hydrogel beads. This incorporation with several interactions can improve the sensitivity, selectively, and treatment profiles via MRI and inductive heating [108,109]. The existence MNPs in a typical drug carrier can enhance the rate of drug release. This is observed in a carboxymethyl chitosan beads [107]. Magnetic polymer beads (MPBs) are categorized under nanocomposite materials. MPBs are composed of nano/micro-particles with almost high magnetization and a polymer texture. The longitude of the beads is in the range of 100 nm to few mm. The MPBs can be synthesized through three well-known procedures;
-
In situ preparation
-
Ex situ preparation
-
Solution interaction

The products of all above mechanisms lead to the formation various structures:
-
Homogeneous/narrow distributions of the magnetic particles in the polymer matrix.
-
Polymer core and magnetic shell (regular core-shell structure)
-
Magnetic core and polymer shell (irregular structure)
-
Regular core-shell in polymer matrix.

Semiconductor (II − VI) quantum dots (QDs), for years now have been used in biomedical applications owing to their remarkable fluorescence performances [115]. Nonetheless, there is an urgent need for a progressive nanomaterial that can substitute with these QDs [116]. Moreover, carbon dots (CDs), unparalleled nanocarbon with dimension <10 nm, are reported in multidisciplinary bio-applications [117]. CDs have been assigned as biomaterials because of their low toxicity, easy synthesis, and biocompatibility [118]. Likewise, the fluorescence characteristic of CDs achieves a great interest in bioimaging [119]. Moreover, CDs are outstanding electron donors and acceptors, which makes them a good candidate for biological detection. In 2014, Cui et al. planned a highly sensitive mercury sensor, which presented a detection limit of 2.6 nM [120]. Dai et al. [121] reported a system based on functionalized amino acid with CDs and gold nanoparticles for detecting melamine in milk. In the similar vein, Liu et al. [122] expressed a biosensor-based CDs for quantitative/qualitative detection of hyaluronidase (hyaluronidase is remarked in metastasis/tumor growth). In addition, CDs-hemoglobin complex is reported by Bui et al. [123] for the detection of cholesterol. In this sensing device, upon addition of cholesterol, a sharp photoluminescence (PL) peak is observed. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have lately received growing attention owing to their strong effects besides quantum confinement [124]. Synthesis of GQDs (based on bottom-up and top-down techniques) and determination of their properties is noteworthy [125,126]. GQDs possess excellent characteristics like huge surface area, extraordinary fluorescence, solubility in various solvents, active functional groups that are located at its edges, the ability of loading drugs by means of π-π interactions as well as synthesis of variable shapes and sizes. GQDs acted as brilliant materials for biomedical applications. Moreover, for cellular imaging application and drug delivery systems, GQDs have been widely examined [127]. Since GQDs basis is wholly composed of “carbon” atoms, they are nontoxic materials in comparison with quantum dots synthesized by inorganic materials that possibly represent toxic ions afterward degradation process [128]. There are many investigations on quantum dots in the form of hydrogel beads potential for medical applications [129].

Hydrogels are broadly presented in many products; nevertheless, the high potential of hydrogels, particularly in modern technologies, has not been clearly investigated/reported. Nowadays, hydrogels are engineered for various applications including (but not limited) tissue, drug delivery, contact lenses, hygiene products, etc. while the biomedical applications are still restricted just in laboratory-scale. In drug-delivery (either devices or scaffolds), though the use of hydrogels is widely investigated, however it is not successfully commercialized. In fact, the limited number of commercial products-based hydrogels are due to their high production cost, and minor side reaction/interaction. In many laboratories, in the recent years, the application of hydrogels in terms of “designing” and “synthesizing” are widely investigated particularly in “anti-cancer drug delivery” and “nanomedical cancer therapy”. Furthermore, magnetic beads suggest an excellent targeted-delivery, where their magnetic responses govern/control the drug-release in low-frequency AFM. This can enable the fundamentals of “environmental protection” and “energy efficiency”. This article is an effort to highlight the various applications of hydrogels for revolutionizing the present research on drug delivery systems specially based on magnetic and targetted drug delivery systems.
Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation; INSF (97017837) and University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant No (159271/33).
